docs.title: How I Built My Data Analytics Portfolio Using MkDocs & Material Theme
docs.text:
Building a personal portfolio is one of the best ways to showcase your data journey, but creating one from scratch can be overwhelming. I wanted a clean, fast, and highly customizable site that holds my projects, vlogs, and resumes without slowing down.

Here is the honest truth: while I am confident in Python and Markdown, I didn't have deep knowledge of CSS or JavaScript. Instead of letting that stop me, I leveraged AI collaboration to code all the custom animations and layout tweaks, while I managed the core architecture, logic, and implementation myself!

docs.title: Technologies & Tools Used
docs.text:
• MkDocs — The fast and lightweight static site generator engine.
  • Material for MkDocs — The premium theme providing a modern, responsive UI.
  • GitHub & GitHub Pages — Used for version control and free public hosting.
  • Python — The backbone for managing packages and environment control.
  • Formspree — Handles the contact form submissions seamlessly without a backend.
  • Giscus — GitHub Discussions-powered comment system injected into the blog.
  • Google Analytics — Integrated tracking paired with a visitor cookie consent dialog.
  • pngquant — Backend image compression via the optimize plugin.

Below is a quick description of what each one does in this project:

docs.title: Video Walkthrough
docs.text: You can watch the quick video overview of my portfolio structure, custom animations, and layout here:

docs.title: Project Description & Step-by-Step Guide
docs.text: Here is the exact step-by-step roadmap showing how this architecture was configured and how you can replicate it.

docs.title: Step 1: Environment & Tool Setup
docs.text:
First, set up an isolated virtual environment using Conda to avoid version conflicts, then install the core packages:

conda create -n mkdocs python=3.13 -y
conda activate mkdocs

pip install mkdocs mkdocs-material
pip install mkdocs-material[imaging]

Important Note for Image Optimization

I downloaded pngquant from its official site, extracted it, and pasted pngquant.exe into C:\Windows\System32\. This allows the optimize plugin to auto-compress image files without throwing errors during site compilation.

docs.title: Step 2: Organizing the Folder Structure
docs.text:
I structured my workspace so adding new projects, resumes, or blogs in the future becomes seamless:

portfolio_of_shahroz/
├── mkdocs.yml                  # Global configuration (plugins, nav, colors)
├── docs/                       # Markdown source files
│   ├── index.md                # Homepage
│   ├── skills.md
│   ├── about.md
│   ├── resume.md
│   ├── contact.md
│   ├── projects/               # Data analyst & electrical project notes
│   │   ├── index.md
│   │   ├── project_1.md
│   │   ├── project_2.md
│   │   └── project_3.md
│   ├── blog/posts/             # My tech vlogs and articles
│   ├── assets/                 # Profile images and local static assets
│   └── stylesheets/
│       └── extra.css           # Custom CSS changes and animations
└── overrides/partials/
    └── comments.html           # Injecting Giscus comment system layout

docs.title: Step 3: Custom Animations & Advanced Features
docs.text:
This is where the magic happened. I used my Python background to handle the site logic, and used AI assistance to build custom CSS animations inside extra.css.

I also configured several built-in Material theme options in my mkdocs.yml:

  • Smart Navigation — Enabled navigation.top (back-to-top button), navigation.tracking (highlights headers as you scroll), and header.autohide to maximize screen space on mobile.
  • Developer Tools — Added content.code.copy so users can instantly copy my code blocks with a single click.
  • SEO & Analytics — Configured custom cookie consent popups linked directly to Google Analytics.

docs.title: Step 4: Deploying to GitHub Pages
docs.text:
After pushing source code changes to the repository, you only need one single command to compile and publish the live site publicly:

# Step A: Push source code to GitHub
git add .
git commit -m "Updated portfolio layout"
git push origin main

# Step B: Compile and publish to GitHub Pages live link
mkdocs gh-deploy

docs.title: Key Takeaways & Lessons Learned
docs.text:
Developing this platform taught me a lot more than just code syntax:

  • AI-Powered Development — It taught me how to combine my core Python/Markdown skills with AI tools to build frontend features (like CSS animations) that I hadn't mastered yet. This is a massive superpower for modern developers!
  • Professional Organization — It forced me to catalog my technical skills and local hardware projects in an organized grid system.
  • Documentation Mastery — Writing clean markdown files taught me how to present complex technical workflows clearly.
  • Showcase My Work Effectively — A live, structured site does more justice to my projects than a static PDF resume ever could.
  • Career Readiness — Building this prepared me for future opportunities by proving I can ship a real, production-deployed product.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
Building this site was a valuable step forward. It functions not just as a portfolio, but as proof of my adaptability and problem-solving mindset. In the real world, what matters is getting things done efficiently, and blending my programming base with AI assistance allowed me to build a premium product.

If you are looking to build your own online presence, I highly suggest trying the MkDocs + GitHub Pages combo. Don't let a lack of web development experience hold you back — use the tools available, start building, and share your journey!

docs.title: My Journey into Data Analytics
docs.text:
I started my career in Technical Support, where I developed problem-solving and troubleshooting skills. While working in support, I discovered my interest in data analysis and automation.

My learning journey included:

  • Python Programming
  • Pandas for Data Analysis
  • SQL & Database Queries
  • Data Visualization
  • Automation Scripts

I am currently building projects and expanding my skills to transition into a data-focused role.

My goal is to work remotely as a Data Analyst or Python Automation Developer and continue learning modern data technologies.

docs.title: Why I Chose Python
docs.text:
Python is one of the most beginner-friendly and powerful programming languages available today.

Some reasons why I enjoy working with Python:

  • Simple syntax
  • Large community support
  • Excellent data analysis libraries
  • Automation capabilities
  • Strong career opportunities

I use Python for data analysis, automation, and personal projects. Its flexibility allows me to quickly turn ideas into working solutions.

Water monitoring system with auto pump control.

👉 View Project

docs.title: 📌 Overview
docs.text:
The Water Turbidity Monitoring System is an Arduino-based embedded project designed to monitor water quality in real time.

It automatically controls a water pump based on turbidity levels and odor detection, ensuring safe and efficient water management through intelligent automation.

The system continuously evaluates water conditions using multiple sensors and triggers safety actions whenever abnormal readings are detected.

docs.title: 🧰 Tools & Components Used
docs.text: 🔌 Arduino Uno 🌊 Turbidity Sensor 🧪 Gas Sensor ⚡ Relay Module 💧 Water Pump 📟 LED Display 💻 C++ (Arduino Programming)

docs.title: 🔄 System Workflow
docs.text:
• 🌊 Turbidity sensor → measures water clarity
  • 🧪 Gas sensor → detects odor / contamination
  • 🧠 Arduino → processes data in real time
  • ⚡ Relay module → controls water pump
  • 🚨 Safety system → stops pump on bad water quality
  • 📟 LED display → shows live status & readings

docs.title: ⭐ Key Features
docs.text:
• 🌊 Real-time monitoring of water quality
  • 🧪 Gas sensor-based odor detection
  • ⚙️ Automated pump control system
  • 🚨 Safety mechanism for automatic shutdown
  • 📟 Live system status display
  • 🤖 Embedded automation using Arduino

docs.title: 📌 Overview
docs.text: A desktop FIR management system that allows users to register and track FIRs online.

docs.title: 🔥 Key Features
docs.text:
• Online FIR registration
  • Case tracking system
  • Duplicate detection (same item in multiple cities)
  • Auto notification system for owner alerts
  • Inter-city record synchronization

docs.title: 💡 Real Example
docs.text: If a bike is stolen in one city and later found in another city: - System detects duplicate entry - Updates status automatically - Sends alert to owner
